Q1: I tried to keep things consistent, you know, setting aside specific blocks of time each week to work through the course materials. With nine online courses, I had to be pretty organized or I'd drown. I usually studied in the evenings after work and on weekend mornings, writing everything down in a planner. It wasn't fancy, but having that routine helped me feel like I was keeping up, even when I wasn't really completing everything.
Q2: I aimed for regular times, but honestly life got in the way more than I'd like to admit. When deadlines piled up, my careful schedule would fall apart and I'd just focus on whatever was most urgent. Being 29 and juggling work and limited resources means I can't always stick to the ideal routine. I tried my best to stay consistent, but some weeks were just survival mode.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about something due or just part of my daily habit to check in. I'd log in during my lunch break at work or first thing when I got home. Sometimes I logged in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I didn't have the energy to really engage. It became almost automatic, like checking email.
Q4: Yeah, logging in became pretty routine after the first few weeks. I'd just do it while having my morning coffee or during breaks. It felt like part of my day, not something I had to think about. The peer support group I was part of helped too because we'd remind each other to check in and stay on track together.
Q5: The video lectures were really helpful because I could watch them while doing other things, like cooking or commuting. I'm a hands-on learner, so anything I could engage with physically or practically stuck better. The discussion boards were useful too when people shared real examples. I watched about 75 minutes of video content which helped me understand concepts better than just reading.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skipped the longer reading assignments and some of the optional quizzes. With limited time and energy, I had to prioritize, and those felt less immediately useful. Sometimes the readings were just too dense and I'd tell myself I'd come back to them, but I rarely did. I also didn't engage much with peer interactions beyond my one study buddy.
Q7: Something felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to what I needed to know for assignments or exams. Practical, applicable stuff that I could actually use. If it felt like busywork or too theoretical, I'd lose interest quickly. I needed to feel like I was making progress, not just going through motions.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones got done because they had to, but optional ones? I'd look at them and think 'maybe later' and later never came. There's something about not having a deadline that makes things feel less urgent, and with everything else on my plate, urgency determined what got done.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials to my phone so I could study on the bus or during slow moments at work. I also used notebooks extensively, writing things out by hand because that's how I learn best. My LMS activity probably doesn't show half of what I actually studied because so much happened offline with printed notes and downloaded PDFs.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd sit with my printed materials and handwritten notes, working through problems without touching the computer. I'd review flashcards I made or practice concepts using physical objects to understand them better. None of that showed up anywhere in the system, but I was genuinely trying to learn. My kinesthetic learning style means I need to do things with my hands.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then work with it for days or weeks without going back online. Storage on my phone was precious but I'd keep the most important stuff there. It was just more practical given my situation, and I didn't always have reliable internet access at home anyway.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. I felt sad a lot during those times, overwhelmed by how much I wasn't completing despite trying. My resilience isn't great, so when things got hard, I'd kind of shut down instead of pushing through. The peer support helped but couldn't fix everything.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or worried about money, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities were constant, and the course often lost that battle. My low resources meant I couldn't always prioritize studying over earning money.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. It looked like engagement but I wasn't absorbing anything. Those moments usually happened when I was too tired but felt guilty about not studying.
Q15: The assignments were the big thing I avoided, which is probably why my completion rate was so low. I'd understand the concepts but freeze up when it came to actually producing work. Part of it was fear of not doing well enough, part of it was just running out of time and energy. I knew they were important but knowing and doing are different things.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd ramp up activity right before something was due and then crash afterward. It wasn't healthy but it was how I functioned. The problem was when deadlines overlapped or I missed one, then everything felt hopeless and I'd disengage even more.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did motivate me to try harder on the next thing. But honestly, with my low assignment completion, I didn't get much feedback to work with. The feedback I did receive made me feel both encouraged and sad about what I could have done better. It's hard when you know you're capable but can't seem to prove it.
Q18: The structure was pretty clear, which I appreciated for keeping my routine. But nine courses meant I couldn't give this one the attention it deserved. The weekly modules helped me know what to expect, but the volume of content across all my courses was just too much. I followed the structure but couldn't keep up with the depth.
Q19: The video content and discussion boards made engagement easier because they fit my learning style and schedule. What made it harder was the sheer amount of reading and the complexity of some assignments. Having clearer, shorter tasks would have helped me actually complete things instead of getting overwhelmed and giving up partway through.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, honestly. You can be clicking around, watching videos, reading posts, really trying to understand, but then freeze when it comes to producing work. Fear of failure, perfectionism, lack of time, or just not having the energy to turn understanding into a finished assignment. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That's not really my experience since I had moderate activity but still failed. But I can imagine someone who studies efficiently offline and just comes to the LMS for essentials. If you're a good independent learner with strong resources, you might not need to be constantly logged in. The LMS isn't the only place learning happens.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns, like when you log in and what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through motions. They definitely can't capture the emotional state behind the numbers or the real-life circumstances affecting your performance.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and making genuine effort to understand it, not just checking boxes. It's feeling connected to the content and wanting to do well. For me, engagement happened in fits and starts, genuine interest mixed with periods of just trying to survive. It's more about the internal experience than external activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied offline with printed materials and handwritten notes. It wouldn't show the sadness I felt about not performing better despite trying. It can't capture my conversations with my peer support buddy or the hours I spent with flashcards on the bus. My engagement was real, even if my results didn't reflect it.
